questionText: Let $n$ be a whole number. Show that '$n$ ends in the digit $0$ or $5$' is a necessary and sufficient condition for '$n$ is divisible by $5$'. You must prove both directions of the implication and state your final conclusion using the symbol $\Leftrightarrow$.

steps.description: Name the two statements to be linked.
steps.workingLatex: P:\ n \text{ ends in } 0 \text{ or } 5, \qquad Q:\ 5 \mid n
steps.explanation: 'Necessary and sufficient' means the two statements imply each other, so our target is $P \Leftrightarrow Q$. Naming them keeps the two directions of the proof clearly separated.

steps.description: Separate a number into its last digit and the rest.
steps.explanation: The last digit $d$ is what 'ends in' refers to, and $10a$ is everything to its left. Splitting $n$ this way lets the single digit $d$ control the whole argument.

steps.description: Note that the leading part is always divisible by 5.
steps.explanation: Ten is itself a multiple of 5, so the entire 'tens and above' part $10a$ is divisible by 5 no matter what $a$ is. This is why only the last digit can decide the outcome.

steps.description: Prove sufficiency: if it ends in 0 or 5 then 5 divides n.
steps.explanation: Assuming $P$, the digit $d$ is itself a multiple of 5, and adding it to the multiple $10a$ keeps the sum a multiple of 5. So $P$ is enough to guarantee $Q$.
Both allowed last digits produce a number equal to exactly 5 times an integer, establishing the sufficiency direction.

steps.description: Prove necessity: if 5 divides n then the last digit is fixed.
steps.explanation: If 5 divides $n$ and also divides $10a$, it must divide their difference, which is the last digit $d$. Divisibility is preserved under subtraction, so this pins down $d$.

steps.description: Solve for the possible last digits.
steps.workingLatex: d \in \{0,\dots,9\} \text{ and } 5 \mid d \Rightarrow d = 0 \text{ or } d = 5
steps.explanation: The only single digits that 5 divides are 0 and 5, so the number must end in one of these. That is exactly statement $P$.
Divisibility by 5 has forced the final digit to be 0 or 5, establishing the necessity direction.

steps.description: Combine both directions into a biconditional.
steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q \ \text{ and } \ Q \Rightarrow P \ \Rightarrow \ P \Leftrightarrow Q
steps.explanation: With both implications proved, the conditions are equivalent: each is both necessary and sufficient for the other.

steps.description: Translate the result into necessary/sufficient language.
steps.workingLatex: P \text{ is necessary and sufficient for } Q
steps.explanation: Because $P \Rightarrow Q$, the digit condition is sufficient; because $Q \Rightarrow P$, it is also necessary. Together this is precisely what the biconditional records.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $P \Leftrightarrow Q$: a whole number ends in $0$ or $5$ if and only if it is divisible by $5$, so the digit condition is both necessary and sufficient.
